name: Swiss Grid (International Typographic)
description: A disciplined style rooted in the International Typographic Style: strict mathematical grids, flush-left ragged-right neutral sans typography, objective hierarchy, asymmetric balance, and a near-total absence of decoration. Order and clarity are the aesthetic.
emotional_impression: Rational, precise, objective, confident, and timeless.
visual_principles: Strict modular grid governing every element's placement
Neutral grotesque typography (Helvetica/Akzidenz lineage) in a tight scale
Flush-left, ragged-right text and clear typographic hierarchy
Functional use of negative space and rule lines
accessibility_risks: Neutral greys chosen for restraint can drift below contrast thresholds
Strict minimalism can under-signal interactive affordances
Tight type scales may force small body sizes
performance_risks: Negligible; the style is structural, not effect-heavy
appropriate_contexts: Products that benefit from a durable, credible, non-trendy foundation
Design-literate audiences who value rigour and clarity
Systems needing a consistent grid across many surfaces
inappropriate_contexts: Brands needing overt warmth, whimsy, or emotional storytelling
Consumer entertainment products where neutrality reads as cold
Contexts demanding strong brand differentiation through ornament
evidence: Müller-Brockmann's 'Grid Systems in Graphic Design' codifies the underlying principles
Its grid-and-restraint discipline remains a low-cliche foundation precisely because it predates and outlasts UI trends
